text: The surgeon performs anterior defect closure using permanent sutures, starting at the bottom of the hernia defect and ensuring overlap behind or below the defect. The sutures catch bits of mesh left in place, and the surgeon uses multiple twelve-inch sutures due to availability. The surgeon imbricates the hernia sac to reduce dead space, parks the needle, and begins closure from the top near the xiphoid process, concerned about tension. The assistant applies pressure on the hernia sac to aid in catching suture bites, with sequential tightening of the sutures.

The surgeon places a new stitch in the center of the hernia defect to offload tension, facilitating closure. As the stitch is cinched, the defect visibly comes together, and the rectus muscles are restored towards the midline. The surgeon continues tightening stitches from the top, ensuring the defect closes smoothly.
The surgeon continues to use each suture until its end to close the hernia defect, ensuring that the cut suture end does not retract back into the muscle, indicating appropriate tension release.

The surgeon continues suturing the hernia sac, ensuring it is imbricated and touches the top of the rectus muscles anteriorly. The linear structure is sutured together with bits of mesh incorporated, using permanent sutures for stability.
The surgeon finishes suturing at the bottom of the hernia repair, addressing a visible gap by placing an additional suture in the middle. The defect appears to be closing well.

The surgeon demonstrates the closure of multiple holes in the left superior side of the posterior sheath using a running 3-0 B-lock suture. The suture effectively gathers the defects, showing a well-aligned closure with no tension. The posterior elements are closed without causing tension on the midline closure.
The surgeon uses extra suture material to close a defect located above the liver, ensuring no uncoated mesh is exposed to the viscera and reducing the risk of postoperative hernia formation.

The surgeon identifies and closes an additional defect in the left upper quadrant, addressing all trocar sites along the left side. The camera view shifts as the surgeon redocks to close one of the defects on the patient's right side.

The surgeon closes the posterior elements starting at the top, docked on the patient's left side. The view is directed towards the xiphoid, with the diaphragm visible in motion. The posterior opening is closed down the midline, with the tissue sitting flat along the viscera, indicating no tension.

The surgeon measures the defect, noting dimensions of approximately forty centimeters in the longitudinal axis and thirty-three to thirty-four centimeters in the horizontal axis. The left upper quadrant port is upsized to twelve millimeters to accommodate the insertion of a Versatex mesh, which is cut to dimensions of thirty-eight to thirty-nine by thirty-three centimeters.

The surgeon unfolds the mesh laparoscopically, adjusting the camera to view both sides of the abdomen. The mesh is carefully unrolled in the right upper quadrant to ensure it lays completely flat without using fixation methods.
The surgeon observes the mesh placement in the retrorectus space, noting its smooth appearance. Drains are left in place following the dissection, and the surgical field is desufflated.

The surgeon closes the left upper quadrant 12mm port site using a suture passer. A drain is visible exiting from the left lower quadrant port, positioned centrally. The surgical field is desufflated, marking the end of the procedure.
